questions.question: A cylindrical metal pipe has an external diameter of $6\text{ cm}$ and an internal diameter of $4\text{ cm}$. Calculate the volume of metal in a pipe of length $25\text{ cm}$ in terms of $\pi$.
questions.explanation:
External radius $= 3$, Internal radius $= 2$. Metal Volume $= \text{Ext Volume} - \text{Int Volume} = \pi(3^2)(25) - \pi(2^2)(25) = 225\pi - 100\pi = 125\pi$.

**Common mistake:** A common error is subtracting the diameters ($6-4=2$) and then finding the volume as if it were a solid cylinder of radius $1$.

questions.question: A spherical ball of radius $3\text{ cm}$ is immersed in water contained in a vertical cylinder of radius $10\text{ cm}$. Calculate the rise in the water level.
questions.explanation:
Rise Volume $= Sphere Volume$2$2\pi(10^{2})h = \frac{4}{3}\pi(3^3)$. $100\pi h = 36\pi$2$h = 36 / 100 = 0.36\text{ cm}$.

**Common mistake:** Students might divide the volume of the sphere by the radius of the cylinder instead of the area of the cylinder's base.

questions.question: A conical beaker has a radius of $8\text{ cm}$ and a height of $15\text{ cm}$. A similar larger conical tank can be filled using the beaker exactly $1728$ times. Work out the radius of the tank.
questions.explanation:
The volume ratio is $1728 : 1$. Since objects are similar, the linear scale factor $k = \sqrt{1728} = 12$. Radius of tank $= 8 \times 12 = 96\text{ cm}$.

**Common mistake:** A student might multiply the radius by 1728 directly or divide by 1728 instead of using the cube root of the volume scale factor.

questions.question: A frustum of a cone has a base radius of $5\text{ cm}$, a top radius of $2\text{ cm}$, and a height of $3\text{ cm}$. Calculate its volume.
questions.explanation:
Total height $H / (H-3) = 5/2 \Rightarrow 2H = 5H - 15 \Rightarrow H = 5$. Large cone $V = \frac{1}{3}\pi(25)(5) = \frac{125}{3}\pi$2$V = \frac{1}{3}\pi(4)(2) = \frac{8}{3}\pi$2$= \frac{117}{3}\pi = 39\pi$.

**Common mistake:** The student might assume the height of the removed cone is the same as the frustum height.

questions.question: Calculate the total surface area of a prism of length $50\text{ cm}$ with a regular hexagonal cross-section of side $10\text{ cm}$ and cross-sectional area $172\text{ cm}^2$.
questions.explanation:
Base area (two hexagons) $= 2 \times 172 = 344$. Perimeter of hexagon $= 60$. Side faces area $= 60 \times 50 = 3000$. Total $= 3344\text{ cm}^2$.

**Common mistake:** A student might only include one hexagonal base or use the area of the hexagon as the perimeter.
